notes: Singular 'you'. The glottal stop (') at the end is a full consonant. In Six Nations/Tyendinaga: ý:se'.
First person singular. The glottal stop (') between the vowels is a full consonant, not punctuation. In Ontario dialects: ý:'i.
Used for a female you respect — a mother, elder, or person of authority. Kanien'kéha encodes respect directly into the pronoun system.
Third person singular male. Ra- marks male throughout Kanien'kéha. In Akwesasne: laónha (r→l alternation).
Three uses: a female animal; a familiar female (sister, close friend); an inanimate object. Context makes the meaning clear.
Dual 'you two'. Kanien'kéha maintains singular/dual/plural as distinct categories.
First person dual inclusive — you and I together.
Dual pronoun for two females.
For two males, or one male and one female together.
Plural 'you all'. The -kwé:kon suffix signals plurality throughout Kanien'kéha.
Inclusive 'we all' — includes the person being spoken to. Contrast with ronwakwé:kon (all of them).
Plural pronoun for all females / a group of women.
Plural pronoun for all males or a mixed group. Ra- marks male/mixed in plural.
Singular female child. I- prefix marks female. The glottal stop (') before the final 'a is phonemically significant.
Female adolescent. The long vowel (:) is critical — shortening it changes the word.
Adult woman. Iak- prefix marks female. The root -kwe appears across Iroquoian languages, related to personhood.
Elder woman — not merely 'old woman'. In Kanien'kehá:ka matrilineal society, elder women hold central ceremonial and social authority.
Singular male child. R- marks male. In Akwesasne: Laksá:'a.
Male adolescent. Ra- prefix marks male. In Akwesasne: Lanekénhteron.
Adult male. Compare Iakón:kwe (woman) — same -kwe root, different gender prefix. In Akwesasne: Lón:kwe.
Elder male. Same -stén:ha suffix as Akokstén:ha (elder woman). In Akwesasne: Lokstén:ha.
Dual female child. The te- prefix marks duality. Compare singular Ieksá:'a (girl).
Dual female adolescent.
Two women. The Teken- prefix marks female dual. Compare singular Iakón:kwe and plural Konón:kwe.
Two elder women (dual form).
Dual male child. Tehni- marks male dual.
Dual male adolescent.
Two men. Compare singular Rón:kwe and plural Ronón:kwe.
Dual elder male form.
Plural female children (3+). Konti- marks female plural. Note the incorporation of -ksa'- (child root) into the word.
Plural female adolescents (3+).
Plural women (3+). Kon- marks female plural for adult person nouns. Compare singular Iakón:kwe, dual Tekenón:kwe.
Plural elder women (3+).
Plural male children (3+). Rati- marks male plural. In Akwesasne: Latiksa'okón:'a.
Plural male adolescents (3+). The -'s suffix marks plurality/habituality here.
Plural men (3+). Ron- marks male plural. In Akwesasne: Lonón:kwe.
Plural elder men (3+).
Maternal kinship term. Kanien'kehá:ka society is matrilineal — clan membership passes through the mother's line. Possessive prefixes change the entire word.
Paternal kinship term. The possessive prefix changes the entire word form.
Grandfather — a term of deep respect. The glottal stop begins the word.
Grandmother. Featured in the Kontinónhstats animated video 'Beading with Tota'. Elder women hold central ceremonial authority.
Your older sister. Kinship terms encode the possessor and the relative age of siblings — English 'sister' carries none of this information.
Your older brother. The Ia-/Ra- prefix alternation (female/male possessor) is consistent across the kinship system.
My daughter. Kinship in Kanien'kéha is relational — the word changes based on whose daughter is being named.
His son. The R-/Ak- prefix alternation marks male/female possessor consistently in kinship nouns.
My grandmother — the possessed form of tota. Aksótha is how you would say this in a sentence ('I am going to my grandmother's house').
My grandfather — the possessed form of 'sot.
Direction question: where are you going? Ka' = where; wáhse' = you go. The direction verb system in Kanien'kéha is rich — 'go', 'come from', and 'went to' are grammatically distinct.
Response to 'where are you going?'. wà:ke = I am going. Used with a destination: 'Ake'nistén:ha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke' (I am going to my mother's house).
Direction question: where did you come from? Distinguishes origin (where you came from) from destination (where you're going).
Direction question about a past action: where did you go? The -skwe' suffix marks past tense in this verb form.

A-class noun (begins with 'a'). The verb incorporation example: áhta' + -owá:nen → watahkowá:nen (a big shoe). This demonstrates noun incorporation, one of Kanien'kéha's most distinctive grammatical features.
A-class noun. The at- prefix is common in A-class nouns and often indicates something worn on the body.
A 'fake' A-class noun — the root meaning 'body cover' is still identifiable. When you can describe the root meaning it usually derives from a verb.
A-class noun for earrings. The ate'- prefix appears in several items worn or attached to the body.
A-class noun for glasses. The atk- cluster is characteristic of several A-class body-related nouns.
A-class noun for chair. Household and everyday object vocabulary.
A-class noun for ball. Note the long vowel (:) before the final syllable.
A 'true' A-class noun — the original root meaning has been lost over time, so only the word survives. This is described in the Kontinónhstats curriculum as an example of how language evolves.
A-stem verb, subjective command form. Structure: s- (you, subjective pronoun) + á:tien (A-stem verb root). Commands never have a suffix because you are directing someone to act.
A-stem verb, first person habitual. Structure: k- (I, subjective) + á:tien (root) + -s (habitual suffix). The -s suffix appears when the action is repetitive.
Dualic A-stem verb — command form. Structure: te- (dualic prefix) + s- (you) + aráhtat (A-stem root). Dualic verbs describe two-phase actions: standing still THEN moving forward.
Dualic A-stem, first person habitual. Structure: te- + k- (I) + aráhtat (root) + -s (habitual). The dualic te- prefix cannot be dropped.
Habitual form — you listen (regularly). Note the at- middle voice infix that appears in reflexive and body-related verbs.
I listen — habitual form. k- is the first-person subjective pronoun in A-stem verbs.
The definite article 'the' in Kanien'kéha. 'ne' marks a specific, identifiable noun. Unlike English, Kanien'kéha articles also signal animacy and social register in more complex sentences.

cite.text: Mithun (1982); Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words; Kontinónhstats curriculum structure

notes: Used before nouns in directional contexts — 'the' when going to get something. From the Lesson 1 example: 'Wá:s skó:ha thi áhta' (Go get the shoe).

cite.text: Kontinónhstats L1 example · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke

notes: Common greeting — 'hello', 'hi', or more literally 'still/yet' (an expression of ongoing life and presence). Used in both casual and formal contexts.
Deep thanks — 'niawenhkó:wa' carries more weight than a simple 'thank you'. It connects to the spirit of the Thanksgiving Address (Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen).

english: That's it / That's right / Well done
notes: Affirming expression — 'that's it', 'that's right', or 'well done'. Used in conversation and teaching to confirm understanding.

english: Wonderful / Excellent (lit. it pleases the mind)
notes: Literally: 'it makes my mind happy/satisfied'. Used to express genuine delight or approval. Demonstrates how Kanien'kéha describes internal states through embodied metaphor.
Mild negation or correction — 'not quite', 'no', or 'that's not right'. Softer than a flat 'no'.
The primary negation particle. Iáh + verb = negated verb. Example: Iáh ká:tiens = I am not sitting. Negation is formed through a particle + verb structure rather than internal verb change.
O-class noun beginning with Ó. Glass and ice share this word — the connection between transparency and cold is embedded in the language.
O-class noun for necklace. Traditional beadwork and wampum are central to Kanien'kehá:ka cultural practice.

O-class noun for money. Ohwísta is mentioned in the Kontinónhstats curriculum opening — 'seeking funding' is expressed using this word. It reflects how Kanien'kéha has adapted to modern economic concepts.
O-class noun for sugar or candy. This word appears in the Lesson 1 curriculum example sentence: 'Shní:non thi otsikhè:ta' (You buy the candy).

cite.text: Kontinónhstats L8 & L1 · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke

notes: O-class noun for water. Water is one of the first beings acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address — 'Onkwehshón:'a ne wáhi ne Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen' opens with gratitude to the waters.
O-class noun for feather. Feathers carry ceremonial significance in Haudenosaunee culture — eagle feathers in particular.
O-class noun for flower. Also appears in the Thanksgiving Address — the flowers of the earth are acknowledged and thanked.
O-class noun for leaf. Part of the nature vocabulary that connects language to the land.
Ka-class noun for car. A loanword concept — Kanien'kéha adapted the noun class system to name modern objects. The Ka- prefix marks this class.

Ka-class noun for paper or book. The root -hiatónhsera is related to writing — kahiatónhsera is literally 'writing-thing'. This is a good example of how Kanien'kéha forms nouns by describing function.
Ka-class noun for door. One of the household vocabulary items in Mary McDonald's curriculum.
Ka-class noun for boat. Watercraft is historically significant for Kanien'kehá:ka people whose territory includes the St. Lawrence River and Lake of Two Mountains.
Ka-class noun for drum. The drum is central to Haudenosaunee ceremony and social dance. Knowing this word connects language to cultural practice.
Ka-class noun for flint or arrowhead. The Haudenosaunee confederacy — the 'People of the Flint' — takes its name from flint. Kanien'kehá:ka means 'People of the Place of Flint'.

The name of the community — often anglicised as 'Oka'. Kanehsatà:ke means 'where the crust of snow on top of the earth is'. The 1990 Oka Crisis centred on this community's defence of their land, including a sacred pine grove. The community's name in its own language carries this history.
The sun — also used in Kanehsatà:ke place and directional vocabulary. The sun appears in the Thanksgiving Address as one of the beings to be thanked.

english: Down the bay / Toward the water
notes: Directional term used in Kanehsatà:ke geography — 'toward the bay' or 'down toward the water'. Place knowledge is encoded in the language.
Red. Colour nouns in Kanien'kéha are often O-class nouns. Onekwénhtara is related to the word for blood — the colour name is descriptive of what is red.
Blue. Oròn:ia is related to the word for sky — blue is the colour of what is above.
Green. Óhonte' is related to the word for grass or vegetation — green is the colour of what grows.
Yellow. The long vowel (:) in Otsì:nekwahr is significant.
Black. One of the basic colour terms.
White. Compare with Ó:wise' (glass/ice) — there may be a shared root related to transparency or paleness.
Brown. The colour of earth and bark.
Pink.
Head. Body part vocabulary in Kanien'kéha is important for learning the middle-voice infix -at- that appears in reflexive verbs (washing one's face, brushing one's teeth, etc.).
Hair — literally 'on the head'. Many body part words in Kanien'kéha are compound forms showing location.
Eye. The root -nen appears in verb forms related to seeing.
Nose. The -yén:ta root is related to smelling and appears in verb forms.
Lips. Connected to speaking and eating verb roots.
Teeth. Appears in the verb 'to brush one's teeth' from the A-stem lesson: satkahnekáhrhons.
Hand. The root -nhstà:'a is incorporated into the verb 'to wash one's hands' from the A-stem verbs: sato'nnenhstáhkwa's.
Arm.
One. Numbers in Kanien'kéha are used for counting, telling time, and expressing quantities. Note that Énska is also the root seen in the word for 'one at a time'.
Two. You have already seen this root in the dual prefix tékeni- (two people). The connection is explicit — duality is built on the number two.
Three. Plural person nouns (three or more) are named relative to this number — the plural threshold.
Four. Also a common root in place names and time expressions.
Five. Wísk is a short, sharp word. Numbers 1–10 form the basis for all counting in Kanien'kéha.
Six. The Haudenosaunee confederacy is made up of Six Nations — Ia:ia'k Onkwehonwe ('six peoples').
Seven.
Eight. The glottal stop (') in sha'té:kon is part of the word — do not drop it.
Nine.
Ten. From ten onwards, numbers are formed by combining roots: eleven = ten + one (óia'k énska), twenty = two tens (tékeni óia'k), and so on.

english: Thanksgiving Address (lit. 'the words that come before all else')
notes: The Haudenosaunee Thanksgiving Address — the most important oral text in Kanien'kéha. Recited at the opening and closing of all gatherings, it acknowledges every element of creation in order. The Kanehsatà:ke dialect version is the basis for the animated film produced by Kontinónhstats.

cite.text: Kontinónhstats animated film · Kanehsatà:ke community; NMAI PDF

notes: 'The people' or 'all human beings' — the first group acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address. Onkwehshón:'a = original/real people.

english: It makes our minds happy / We give thanks
notes: The closing refrain of each section of the Thanksgiving Address — 'and so our minds are made happy' or 'and so we give thanks'. The same root as Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne ('it makes my mind happy') but in the first person plural inclusive.
From the Thanksgiving Address — acknowledging the earth. Ohwén:tsia = earth/ground; Ionkwarihó:ten = it takes care of us / it is our responsibility.
Star — also appears in the night sky vocabulary of the Thanksgiving Address. The stars are the last beings acknowledged before the Creator.
Rectangle — Ka-class shape noun.
Square — the te- prefix is the dualic prefix you already know, here marking equal sides.
Oval — Ka-class shape noun.
Triangle — literally 'three sides'. You already know Áhsen (three) from the numbers lesson. Shape names in Kanien'kéha describe the shape's properties, not just name it.
Circle — te- prefix + noun root. The circle is a sacred shape in Haudenosaunee tradition, representing the continuous cycle of life.
Diamond — literally 'four sides'. Kaié:ri (four) + nikahióhsake (sides). Another numerically-descriptive shape name.
Sun — acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address as 'our eldest brother who carries his face across the sky each day'. You first encountered this word in the Kanehsatà:ke place names lesson.

Bear — one of the three Mohawk clan animals. The Bear Clan (Rotihskeré:wake) is one of the three clans of the Kanien'kehá:ka. The bear appears in the Tóta tánon Ohkwá:ri puppet show produced in Kahnawà:ke.
Turtle — one of the three Mohawk clan animals. The Turtle Clan (Rotiniáhton). In Haudenosaunee creation story, Turtle Island (North America) is formed on the back of a great turtle.
Wolf — one of the three Mohawk clan animals. The Wolf Clan (Ronathahí:non). The three original Mohawk clans are Bear, Turtle, and Wolf.
Beaver. Historically important as a trade animal — the beaver pelt trade dramatically affected Kanien'kehá:ka life and politics in the 17th and 18th centuries.
Wild rabbit. A common animal in the Kanehsatà:ke region.
Deer. Venison is a traditional food; deer hides were used for clothing. The deer appears in clan and ceremony vocabulary.
Moose — the largest land animal in the Kanehsatà:ke territory. The St. Lawrence lowlands and Laurentians have been moose territory for Kanien'kehá:ka hunters.
Porcupine. Porcupine quills are used in traditional quillwork — an art form predating European beads.
Cat. Domestic animals arrived with European contact — their names in Kanien'kéha were created using the language's own word-formation rules, not borrowed directly.
Horse — literally related to 'it carries on its back'. The horse arrived with European contact in the 17th century; the word describes the animal's function rather than borrowing a foreign name.
Cow. Another post-contact domesticated animal. Compare Tiohnhónhskwaron rá:tsi'n (bull) — the bull is named as the male form of the cow.
Goat. Ka-class domestic animal noun.
Sheep — literally 'it has wool on its back'. Descriptive compound: the animal is named for its defining characteristic. This is Kanien'kéha's productive word-formation at work.

Bear Clan — one of the three original Mohawk clans. Clan membership is matrilineal: you belong to your mother's clan. The Bear Clan is known in oral tradition for its strength and its role as healers.
Turtle Clan — one of the three original Mohawk clans. The Turtle Clan is associated with leadership and is connected to Turtle Island, the Haudenosaunee name for North America.
Wolf Clan — one of the three original Mohawk clans. The Wolf Clan is associated with pathfinding and trail-keeping — those who know the way.

Snipe Clan — originally an Onondaga clan present in Kanehsatà:ke due to the historical resettlement of Oswegatchie Onondaga people by 1806. The presence of non-Mohawk clans at Kanehsatà:ke is part of the community's specific history.
Eel Clan — also originally Onondaga, present at Kanehsatà:ke through the same historical resettlement. The Eel Clan's presence at Kanehsatà:ke reflects the community's unique composition.
Strawberries — one of the first foods of the spring season, celebrated in the Strawberry Ceremony. Strawberries hold a special place in Haudenosaunee ceremonial life as the first fruit of the year.
Cranberries — a traditional food of the Kanehsatà:ke region, found in the bogs and wet areas near the community.
Raspberries — a summer fruit gathered in the forests and clearings of the Kanehsatà:ke area.
Cherries. The cherry tree is also part of traditional medicine knowledge.
Blueberries — abundant in the region and important in traditional diet. Blueberries are gathered in late summer.

Apples. Apple orchards were significant at Kanehsatà:ke — the 1990 Oka Crisis was partly triggered by a golf course development that would have destroyed orchards and a sacred pine grove on traditional territory.
Potatoes. An important staple food in modern Kanien'kehá:ka households.
Meat — general term. Wild game (deer, moose, rabbit) has been central to Kanien'kehá:ka diet alongside the Three Sisters.
Butter — a post-contact food item, named using Kanien'kéha word-formation principles rather than borrowed directly.

Beans — one of the Three Sisters (corn, beans, squash), the foundation crops of Haudenosaunee agriculture. The Three Sisters are companion plants: corn provides the trellis, beans fix nitrogen, squash shades the ground.
Corn — the most sacred of the Three Sisters. Corn is central to Haudenosaunee ceremony, agriculture, and creation stories. The word appears in compound forms throughout the vocabulary.
Squash — the third of the Three Sisters. Together, corn, beans, and squash (Onón:ta, Ohsahéta', Otá:ra) form the agricultural and ceremonial cornerstone of Haudenosaunee culture.
Bread. Corn bread (onón:ta kanà:taros) is a traditional food.
Bear meat — a compound noun: Ohkwá:ri (bear) + o'wá:ron (meat). This demonstrates how nouns combine in Kanien'kéha. You already know both component words.

Longhouse — the traditional communal dwelling and the social/political unit of Haudenosaunee life. The Haudenosaunee Confederacy is called 'People of the Longhouse'. Clan mothers governed the longhouse.

english: The Great Law of Peace / The Good Path
notes: The Great Law of Peace — the constitution of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy, brought by the Peacemaker. Kaià:ner means 'good path' or 'good way'. Haudenosaunee political philosophy expressed in this law influenced the US Constitution according to some historians.

Wampum belt — teioháte. Wampum belts are records of treaties, alliances, and oral agreements. The Kanehsatà:ke Two Dog Wampum Belt was returned from the McCord Stewart Museum in 2024 — a major act of cultural repatriation. See the news item on the Kontinónhstats website.

Peace — central to Haudenosaunee identity. The Peacemaker (Tekanawí:ta) brought Skennón:'a, Kasastensera (strength/righteousness), and Kanikonhri:io (good mind) as the three principles of the Great Law.
Native tobacco — used ceremonially, not recreationally. Tobacco carries prayers upward to the Creator. It is placed in fire, water, or the earth as an offering and thanks.

Corn husk doll — a traditional toy and cultural object made from dried corn husks. According to Haudenosaunee tradition, the corn husk doll has no face — she gave her face to the people and was taught humility.
Soap. Household vocabulary shows how Kanien'kéha has adapted to modern domestic life through its own word-formation system.
Bed — literally related to 'where one rests'. The -yentha' suffix is a locative indicating the place of the action.
Towel or face cloth. Body-care vocabulary connects to the A-stem verbs you learned (washing face, brushing teeth).
Moon — acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address as our Grandmother Moon who governs the tides and the female cycle. The moon is feminine in Haudenosaunee tradition.
Earth — the same root you saw in 'flower' (Ohwén:tsia). The earth and what grows from it share a root — the language encodes their relationship.
Maple — the tree of the Kanehsatà:ke region and a cornerstone of the sugar bush tradition. Maple sap (ohsawén:ton wahta) is gathered each spring in a ceremony connecting the community to the land.
River — literally 'on the river' or 'by the water'. The St. Lawrence River (Kaniatarà:ke) is central to Kanehsatà:ke geography and history. The Lake of Two Mountains is nearby.
Mountains — literally 'where they are tall'. The Blue Mountain (Karáhkwa, in local usage) is visible from Kanehsatà:ke and is part of the community's geographic identity.
Winter. The four seasons in Kanien'kéha reflect the agricultural and ceremonial calendar of Haudenosaunee life.
Spring — the season of renewal, first foods ceremonies, and the return of the maple sap. The Strawberry Ceremony follows shortly after.
Summer. The growing season — corn, beans, and squash are tended through the summer months.
Autumn — the harvest season. The Green Corn Ceremony and Harvest Ceremony mark this time of year.

The Peacemaker — the founder of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy who brought together five warring nations under the Great Law of Peace. He is not named directly in many Haudenosaunee communities out of respect; here we use the respectful form Tekanawí:ta.

Hiawatha (Aiionwátha) — the Mohawk chief who worked alongside the Peacemaker to unite the nations. Not to be confused with Longfellow's fictional 'Hiawatha' — the historical Aiionwátha is a Haudenosaunee political figure of immense importance.

Longhouse Chief — a male chief selected by the clan mother of his clan. Chiefs serve at the pleasure of the clan mothers and can be removed ('dehorned') if they do not serve the people well. This selection system is part of the Great Law.

Clan Mother — the most important political figure in the Haudenosaunee system. Clan mothers select and remove chiefs, hold clan membership records, and are the keepers of the lineage. The language and the political system speak together here.

The Creator — acknowledged last in the Thanksgiving Address. Shonkwaiá:tison = 'the one who created us'. The Creator is named at the very end of the Address, after all of creation has been acknowledged.
Singers — Ratiwennahní:ion. Song and ceremony are inseparable in Haudenosaunee tradition. Social dances, condolence ceremonies, and ceremonial cycles all have distinct songs.
